facts.text: Laurel Hill earned its name in 1895 when a towering laurel tree stood at the town’s center, inspiring the new name after the original Almirante.
The Yellow River Railroad reached Laurel Hill in 1892, sparking a lumber boom that linked the town to the wider Florida Panhandle.
Laurel Hill officially incorporated in 1905, becoming Florida’s northernmost incorporated city—a title it still holds today.
Every October, the town celebrates its quirky heritage with the Laurel Hill Hobo Festival, first held in 1992 on the first Saturday.
Historic churches line Laurel Hill’s streets, including South Ebenezer Baptist, Laurel Hill Presbyterian, and Mount Zion Baptist, reflecting its deep religious roots.
